title: Semantic Search Overview
text: Semantic search retrieves information by meaning instead of exact keyword overlap. Documents and user questions are converted into dense vector embeddings, and results are ranked by similarity in embedding space. This helps users find relevant material even when they use different wording than the original document.

title: Embedding Model Selection
text: Embedding models should be selected based on domain, latency, language coverage, cost, and retrieval quality. A small model such as all-MiniLM-L6-v2 is useful for demos and low-latency prototypes, while larger domain-specific models can improve recall for technical or legal corpora.

title: Document Ingestion Pipeline
text: A production ingestion pipeline validates source documents, normalizes text, splits content into chunks, generates embeddings, writes index artifacts, and records metadata about the dataset and model version. Each run should be reproducible so a team can rebuild the same index later.

title: Chunking Strategy
text: Chunk size affects retrieval quality. Very small chunks can lose context, while very large chunks can include unrelated information and reduce precision. A practical starting point is 300 to 800 tokens with modest overlap, then tune the values using evaluation queries.

title: Vector Store Backends
text: Vector stores keep embeddings and support nearest-neighbor retrieval. Local FAISS works well for demos and batch-built indexes. Larger systems often use Qdrant, Chroma, Milvus, pgvector, OpenSearch, or managed vector search so the index can scale and be updated independently of the API service.

title: Retrieval Evaluation
text: Retrieval quality should be measured with labeled queries. Recall at K tells whether at least one relevant document appears in the top results. Mean reciprocal rank rewards systems that place the first relevant result near the top. Evaluation should run after every model, data, or chunking change.

title: RAG Answer Grounding
text: Retrieval augmented generation grounds an answer in retrieved context. The retriever selects relevant chunks, and the generator should answer only from those chunks. The response should include sources so a user can inspect the evidence and decide whether the answer is trustworthy.

title: FastAPI Serving Pattern
text: FastAPI is a good fit for serving retrieval systems because it provides request validation, automatic OpenAPI documentation, and simple deployment with Uvicorn. Expensive resources such as embedding models and vector indexes should be cached rather than loaded on every request.

title: API Health and Readiness
text: A service should expose a lightweight health endpoint and a stricter readiness endpoint. Health confirms the web process is alive. Readiness confirms dependencies such as index files are present and the application can accept traffic.

title: Docker Deployment
text: Docker makes the RAG service portable by packaging Python dependencies, source code, and index-building steps. A common approach is to build the vector index during the image build for small corpora, then run Uvicorn inside the container. Larger indexes are usually restored from persistent storage.

title: Cloud Deployment Options
text: A small FastAPI RAG service can be deployed on Render, Fly.io, Railway, AWS App Runner, Google Cloud Run, or Azure Container Apps. The platform should support container builds, environment variables, enough memory for the embedding model, and a readiness health check.

title: Model Caching
text: Sentence Transformer models are downloaded from Hugging Face if they are not already cached. Production deployments should either allow the download during build time, pre-cache the model in the image, or point the embedding configuration at a local model directory.

title: Observability for Search
text: Search observability should record query latency, top K, result counts, backend type, and errors. Teams often log anonymized queries and no-result cases to improve evaluation coverage and discover content gaps in the knowledge base.

title: Security and Privacy
text: RAG services should avoid indexing secrets, credentials, personal data, and documents the application is not authorized to expose. For private corpora, retrieval must enforce access control before returning chunks or using them in an answer.

title: Index Versioning
text: Index artifacts should include metadata such as embedding model name, chunk size, chunk overlap, document count, chunk count, backend, and build time. This lets teams compare evaluations across versions and roll back if a new index performs poorly.

title: Handling Noisy Documents
text: Real documents often include boilerplate, navigation text, tables, broken formatting, and repeated footers. Ingestion should clean obvious noise and preserve important headings. Better text quality usually improves retrieval more than changing the vector database.

title: API Error Handling
text: The API should return clear errors when the index is missing or the embedding model cannot be loaded. A production service should fail readiness checks until required artifacts exist instead of accepting traffic and failing during user requests.

title: Evaluation Dataset Design
text: Evaluation queries should reflect real user language, not just document titles. A useful set includes direct questions, paraphrases, ambiguous queries, and operational questions. Each query should list the relevant document IDs expected in the top results.

title: Scaling Beyond a Demo
text: To scale beyond a demo, separate ingestion from serving, move the vector index to a persistent backend, track evaluation metrics, add authentication, monitor latency, and automate deployment. These steps make the system easier to operate as data grows.

title: Resume Project Positioning
text: A strong semantic search resume project should show more than a notebook. It should include ingestion, evaluation, API serving, Docker deployment, configuration, tests, and clear documentation about tradeoffs and next steps.
